steps.text: Prepare a sampling validation plan that encompasses all aspects of the validation activity and follows a standardized format.
Ensure that the new collection method is consistent with the planned analytical methods to be utilized.
Document and either limit the variables tested or ensure that a range of conditions are included in the test.
Document the process by which the samples are collected.
Conduct the initial controlled validation test.
Test multiple locations and conditions in controlled environments.
Repeat some or all aspects of the study.

preconditions.text: Procedural changes are required due to troubleshooting and optimization.

constraints.text: Safety concerns and procedures must be addressed in both the sampling method and the validation plan, including personal protective equipment and first aid.
Address physical hazards and chemicals that are toxic, corrosive, emit harmful or explosive vapors, or that are incompatible when mixed.

steps.text: Identify and address interferences during the development of the sampling validation plan.
Evaluate selectivity by sampling an appropriate environmental media both with and without the expected interferences.
Sample multiple environments containing varying (and measured) amounts of gravel to allow for comparison and evaluation.
Address chemical sampling devices versus physical sampling devices when evaluating sampling method selectivity.
Select sampling equipment and methods considering material compatibility, chemical compatibility, sample volume capabilities, physical requirements, ease of operation, and decontamination.

preconditions.text: If a soil sampling technique is impacted by the amount of gravel contained in the sample environment

constraints.text: Ensure material compatibility of equipment including gloves, mixing pans, knives, and sample containers.

steps.text: Consider the uncertainties involved with the actual analytical measurement of the sample, including parameters such as analytical instrumental sensitivity, instrument calibration, analytical duplicate analysis, spike sample analysis, method bias (including sample preparation and instrumental analyses), and matrix interferences.

Consider the uncertainties involved in collection of the sample in the field, including parameters such as taking an adequate number of field samples to adequately portray environmental conditions at the sampling site, spatial homogeneity issues, adequate preservation of samples (chemical and temperature preservation), and sampling equipment and techniques.

preconditions.text: Determine the actual use of the measurement, as the depth of looking at uncertainty considerations depends on this use (e.g., if only gaining a general understanding of local environmental conditions, a precise representation of total uncertainty may not be needed).

steps.text: Perform ruggedness testing using experimental designs to examine method performance when minor changes are made in operating or environmental conditions.

Evaluate aspects of sample integrity, including temperature control, preservation chemicals, storage, container compatibility, labeling/seals, logbooks, chain of custody, and transportation regulations.
Include appropriate QC steps to verify sample integrity, such as introducing a gas of known value along with sample gas matrix and observing recovery.
Determine the appropriate sample size, either by extensive mixing of sub-samples to form one sample or by collecting a grab sample with a device with minimal sample size.

preconditions.text: Direct measurements are made in the field

constraints.text: Protect the sample from physical contamination, loss of volume, chemical reactions, volatilization, or temperature change.
Volume and weight requirements depend on the target analyte(s), the analytical method that will be used, and the data requirements.

steps.text: Establish and verify the validity of the sampling method by field studies.
Prepare a sampling method validation report suitable for placing in the public docket.

constraints.text: The validation report must address the topics outlined in the guidance document, providing background information, validation techniques, changes made, and recommendations.
At the minimum, the sampling method validation report must address the information contained in Table 1.

Validation studies for new sampling technologies should take into account the routine sample size typically collected and submitted to the laboratory, especially when a composite sample is not feasible.
A discussion of safety concerns and procedures should be addressed, including personal protective equipment and first aid.
Additional concerns include physical hazards and chemicals that are toxic, corrosive, emit harmful or explosive vapors, or that are incompatible when mixed.

steps.text: Provide a discussion of the defined quality control/quality assurance (QA/QC) checks and guidances.
Discuss method development.
Discuss results of the validation testing and an evaluation including comparison with the reference substances and preparations, acceptance criteria and recommendations.
Note any changes made to the sampling method as a result of the validation studies.
Discuss formal acceptance/rejection of work.
Provide recommendations for future work.

preconditions.text: Before publication, EPA sampling methods for chemical and radiochemical parameters are either peer reviewed in accordance with the information provided in the most current version of the EPA Science Technology Policy Council’s Peer Review Handbook (1) or reviewed by a documented alternate procedure that ensures the robustness and technical viability of the method while considering stakeholder and expert input.

steps.text: Include the sampling validation plan as a section within the plan or as a stand-alone document attached as an appendix.
Integrate the contributions and requirements of all stakeholders and present this information in a clear, concise format.
Conduct validation planning as part of initial planning (e.g., directed planning process).
Communicate the information and documentation identified in the validation plans to the laboratory as part of the Statement of Work (SOW).
